channel.title: +Articles on Vishvas's notes
channel.description: Recent content in +Articles on Vishvas's notes

item.title: Muslim demographics 2024
item.description: Source: TW Muslim socio-economic indicators are ahead of SCs across India. Except in the Hindi heartland, Muslim socio-economic indicators are on par with OBC numbers across India. As far as the TFR is concerned, while the gap is narrowing, there are no signs that Hindu and Muslim TFRs will ever converge, so the trend of Muslim share of population increasing is due to continue for the next 30 years at least.

item.title: nehru-envin-pact
item.description: Nehru, like a typical imperialist slave, signed an agreement with Verier Envin, a foreign born missionary, imagine, to block Hindu Sadhus from parts of NE. Not just that, on Verier’s orders Nehru followed a policy of isolating NE from rest of India so Verier’s missionaries could convert all of NE and persecute Hindus in peace. The shameless man allowed the depraved missionaries to exploit the tribals too. Verier himself got sexually involved with underage women there.

item.title: nehru-muslim-reinvitation
item.description: If they were indeed to be regarded as part of the “body and spirit” of Pakistan, could the Pakistani state legitimately restrict their entry? The Nehru-Liaqat Pact provided the Pakistani state with an agreement which appeared to secure Muslim rights within India, and could be used to argue that as a result there was no imperative for Muslim flight from India to Pakistan. As part of the agreement, the Indian government agreed to take back, in addition to the Muslims displaced in the east, Muslims from UP who had left for Pakistan between February I and May 31, 1950, and restore their properties to them.

item.title: Sikh separatist triggers
item.description: State reorganization issue Nehru’s betrayal of Partition-era promises to Sikhs regarding “a territorial area within the nation to develop its own life and culture (…) with a great deal of autonomy for its constituent units.” (October 1945; reiterated in July 1946). Sikhs had lost a great deal and felt a need to culturally consolidate within India. Operation Blue Star was not the first time the Golden Temple Complex was assaulted post-Independence.
